speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four, May 31, 1996. And our survivor is Mr. Sol Glick. So you arrived in America in September 1949. And-- did you go to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went straight to Philadelphia because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They hire us, arranged in Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you went straight to the gentleman who had hired you for the photography studio?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, two days waiting, I went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a--

speaker: interviewee
text: As a janitor, sweeping the floor. I didn't speak English. The boss speaks Jewish. And I-- and the friend's boss, who was on the studio, speaks Russian. But I didn't speak Russian. I speak Jewish to them. After three months, he laid me off. Just like this. He laid me off. I had no money, nothing. I was working hungry. I had-- he thinks I have money. I was from Germany, when [INAUDIBLE] money. I didn't have no money, nothing. I got sick. I almost died from hunger. I didn't have $0.10 to get a [INAUDIBLE] to look for a job. Where I come, they say you don't work enough. You cannot get unemployment. Anyway, and I went to the hospital. A policeman took me to the hospital. I lived with my neighbor. I had a room there, to Mount Sinai Hospital in Philadelphia. I had pneumonia. I almost die.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came in. They put me in a room. And I-- and I was burning up from fever. And people were waiting to go in, like an appointment to the doctor. And I says to the nurse, why don't you let me in? I'm dying. Oh, no. You have to wait. And I walked into the doctor. He give it to the nurse. The man is almost die. These people come for a checkup. Why don't you bring him in? And he said to me, all right. You go home and take it easy. He gave me some medicine. When I walked out from the doctor, I collapsed. I collapsed. They rush me back to-- to the room. And I was on bed [? sheet. ?] The first thing they ask me, who is gonna pay the bill? I said, call the HIAS. And the HIAS told them, we are gonna pay. Keep him there. Keep him there till he recuperates. You know what I mean? We are gonna pay. After I was discharged, they said to me, you have to come home. You have good nourishment. I said, where? I live by-- an elderly couple in a room. Nourishment where? And then-- and there was a colored woman there sweeping the floors. And she saw nobody come to visit me. She said to the doctor, I take him home. That's right. She wants to take me home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no, no. I go home. And a woman was-- a neighbor there, a Jewish woman who has a Jewish butcher store. And she was very nice. And then she forced me to come in to have a dinner there. The food was nothing, and I didn't want it. And she forced me to come in. And she saw I don't have nothing to eat. But I-- I did any kind of work for her. I would not want charity to eat. It was very nice people. When I got married, they were invited to the wedding and everything. And I think, if not for them, this is true. I wrote a letter to Truman in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wrote a letter. And I said, look. I lived through the war. I came to America. Food is wasted, and I'm starving. Why don't you send me back to any place you wanted? I don't want to die here. The HIAS got the letter. I gave it to HIAS to mail it. And they called my boss, and they tell him, you could be arrested. You brought him to America, guaranteed a job. And you have so many people employed, and you laid him off? I didn't know you don't have money. I said, what, I have to report to you? I want a job. And the HIAS said to me. They're gonna give me $25. I was making $20 a week. And he said to me, $0.50 an hour. And he says, all right, I give you some money. Maybe we find you a job. I said, I don't want money. I want a job. I don't want charity. And the boss said to me, all right. Come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since then, I went back. I worked twice as hard. I used to sneak in, in the dark room, when the people were working, photographers. I never told them. I finish my job cleaning and everything. I sneaked into the dark room. Later when he need some people, printers, and I learned the trade better with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were watching.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't work. I just watched them. And he needed to finish the job, first the schools and everything. I said, how about me learning? What you? You don't know nothing. I said, let me go in. I show you. Give me a job. And I made the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since then, he was shocked. How did you learn? Later on, he said-- and-- and I organized everything. I told him, a big place, how to keep the files, how do anything. And he said, you know what? I'm now gonna make you the manager. I said, you crazy? You're going to make me the manager. I said, how much I'm going to make? Oh, we give you a raise. After I got married, I was the manager already there. I said, what are you doing to me? You give me $1,000 bonus Christmas. I work day and night, overtime, with everything, I didn't get pay overtime. When I was at work, I get pay overtime. He said, so what are you doing? You took off $250 tax. I just got $750. Why don't you put $1,250, and give me cash $1,000? Oh no, I cannot do it. I said, you not do it, I quit.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, all right. I give you. And it was another man there at the studio, who-- who was a manager there before, working with him. He quit. He opened a studio, a big studio you just like him. And people who quit for our job went there, the same, Philadelphia. And they told him, a boy came from Europe. And he's a manager at [INAUDIBLE]. He's one of the best. He runs the studio with everything and you can't believe it. He's a-- anything. And this man needs somebody to replace his son-in-law as the manager, to go out to get schools and jobs. He always asked me to come over. I said, I don't want it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked my boss before I took my vacation, give me $10 a raise. He cannot afford it. I said, you should live so long. I'm running the studio. I know what you are making. You're going tell me you cannot afford it? What is it with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took my job. And somebody was friends for there told me, I should go out to [INAUDIBLE] studio.

speaker: interviewer
text: To who? To the other studio?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the studio, [INAUDIBLE]. You vacation, see-- he always asks people who worked with me to make me come up. He wants to talk to me. He was in Atlantic City on vacation. I went up. And the people who worked with me before saw me coming up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, Sol, what are you doing? I said, I'm the manager. How much you make? I swear to God, I was ashamed to tell him. I told him double this salary. He said, I give you the same salary. Listen to this. He's gonna give me $10. He give me the double salary. How much you get vacation? I said, three weeks. You get three weeks too. Was that part true?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you really get three weeks from the original guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wait a minute. And he said, I give you the vacation. I double your bonus. He was there, and the manager took me into the dark room to print. He gave me a an order. In 10 minutes, I finished all of it. He called up the boss in Atlantic City. Come back. You've got a man who can never get this kind of person. This way he can take over, and I go out to the street. He came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the place that you-- you were working at?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the other place.

speaker: interviewee
text: The place where I went out there. And I-- I was three weeks vacation. He came back, and he had a conference with me, the boss. And I said, I hear everybody who works for you tell you are a rotten boss. You are impossible to get along, and you fire a lot of people. I said, so what are you going to do when you fire me, and you hire me, and I lost this job? I like the boss. I never talked about him. But he gave me-- I maked a living. I liked him. But you never talked about the boss.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me, so what are you going to do? You want to stay with me? I said, look, I'm gonna to answer you. I'm going to go back, and I'm going to give my boss two weeks-- Notice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Notice. I'm not going to leave like this. And I'm gonna tell them if it takes more till he breaks in somebody on my place, then I'm leaving. The people already told my boss I went in to [INAUDIBLE] studio. And when I come back in the morning from vacation, 8 o'clock I open. Before 8:00 o'clock the people start. 8 o'clock I'm preparing the work for them. He's already in the studio, the boss. He never comes. 10 o'clock here's there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He calls me up on the phone, Sol, come into my office. I hear you went up to [INAUDIBLE]. I said, yes. So what you do? I said, I ask you a question. I ask you $10 a raise, you said you cannot afford it. I lied to [INAUDIBLE], and I told him, I doubled the salary what you give to me, and he agreed. So if you were in my-- Shoes, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --position what you do? You leave? He said, yes. Sure, I take the job. I said, I took it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He almost passed out. You leave me in this situation before Christmas? You know, after Labor Day we start to work hard to finish the schools, the yearbooks, and everything. You leave me? You cut my hands? I said, look, just a minute. You didn't treat me right. I worked for you 30 years, day and night. So it's like mine. 30 years this was already?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for him for 30 years, the boss, till I decided to quit. Because of the other studio. And I said, well, you didn't treat me right. I was married and everything. I struggled. And-- and everything. He said to me, why don't you talk over to your wife. We want you-- I gave her some with [INAUDIBLE] already. Now you can give me double the-- the salary?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came home. I said, don't mix in my wife. She's not working with you. It's me. I come home from work. He and his wife were there at my house, talking to my wife. And I asked the people, friends of mine, what do you do in a situation like this? She said, you've got nothing against him? I said, no. He just used me. He pay me starvation-- starving salary. What will you do? Look, he gave you the same salary? He agreed to give you a better situation? Don't switch. If you have not given-- and I told him, I'm staying. I went out to the boss there, at [INAUDIBLE]. I told him, look, I'm sorry. I cannot leave him, and I'm staying. And he said, Sol, you don't have to tell me nothing. Whenever you decide to come in, you got a job here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked a couple of years. The children came in to work. Oh, they graduate college, they were little babies. And they become my bosses too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who? His children?

speaker: interviewee
text: His children, they get out of college, engineers, and this, all them come to the business, the wives. I said, I cannot handle it no more. If-- and I said to the oldest one, I said, I'm gonna leave. He said, you're leaving? I said, yes. I'm leaving. I said, you sign my slip. And my brother got sick at the time too of cancer. I said, I'm going to California. We're not going to give you unemployment. You quit. I said you should live so long. I went in, and I got unemployment. And I went there to California.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before you tell me about California, you have to tell me-- tell us, just a little bit about your lovely wife, and her name, and when-- where you met her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I met her through introduction.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in 19-- in 1952, I met her. No. In '52, we almost-- in 1952, yeah, '52, yes. I met her at the end of '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: An American girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: An American girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. See, I used to go for the Jewish holiday to New York. My friends who know me from Europe, they came to America for the Jewish holiday tradition, we have the New Years, and this. And my wife lived in the same building with-- with-- with these people. She had no father. She was the only child. Her father die young. And they had a daughter. They were girlfriends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother-in-law used to go in, Mrs. [? Geller, ?] maybe you got a nice boy for this and everything. And she bothered me all the time to call my wife, for three years I had her telephone. I said, Mrs. [? Geller, ?] she's an American, graduated college, and I'm a [NON-ENGLISH] from Europe. I didn't speak English perfect either, nothing. Why was she going to go for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my landlady, I used to go dancing, I was good looking, and I have a lot of girlfriends.

speaker: interviewer
text: They say you look like Gene Kelly, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right. And every girl ask me, who don't you get married? Why is a guy like you not married? I said, why a girl like you not married? And I used to go with a lot of girls. They used to call constantly. And I was not ready to get married. I didn't make a living. I work for this boss. And they were thinking, a photographer makes a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are already past 30?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I was full of life. And-- and-- what-- one girl I met, and I took her home. And she was in love like crazy, and she showed me her book. She lived in Philadelphia. She had a home. She wants to bring my brother from Israel, and give him an apartment and this. And she was older than me. And I-- I would not-- no way I would marry her. I want to have children, without children, I don't want it. She showed me the book, and the house and everything. I quit. And I was afraid I [? sleep ?] there, but then I have clothes there. I was afraid some Americans are going to give me problems. I took the policeman with me. He put on civilian clothes, and we went there. And she didn't say nothing. I took my clothes out. She wind up in hospital anyway. But anyway, I met my wife. I came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name? What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Miriam.

speaker: interviewee
text: I come there to visit, happy New Year's. And she gives me the telephone. Some girl wants to talk to you. I started to talk to her. And she said, I just came back from Florida. I was in Florida. She said, I go to Florida all the time. Where we were staying? And this. And I made a date with her the following week to go. And I used to work seven days. I said, I'm gonna see you. My brother was already in-- in-- in New York. I went to see him, and I went to see. He lived in New York with his wife and children, in the Bronx. And I said to him, I'm coming. I take a bus after work on a Friday night. I took the bus. It was on Thanksgiving. It was a snowstorm you'll never believe. I got stuck. Instead of arriving at 7 o'clock, I arrive at 1 o'clock in the morning. I went up to my brother, and I called her. She said, she'll waiting. I took the-- the subway, and I went there to Yonkers. She lives in Yonkers at the time. And I took the bus. I came over. I rang the bell. Her mother open. She went in, a one-bedroom apartment. She was dressed, eyes beautiful, beautiful girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in there and stayed, I said, what are you going to do? Let's go out. I work seven days. I said, let's go out because of Thanksgiving. And we saw a picture, movies. I think the movie was the movies. Then we went to a restaurant and had coffee. And I went back about 3 or 4 o'clock, took her home. And her mother called up. Why don't you send him home? He's not for you. Who said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, he's not for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's not for you. What are you going to-- why don't you send him home. She's said, Mom, keep quiet. And I left there. And I went right back to-- to work. I didn't sleep, at night time, Sunday I work. Saturday I worked. And later on, she liked me. And I went back the following week for a date. And I told her, look. I like you, everything, but I can offer you nothing. I'm a hard-working man. And my brother used to tell to me, if you bring in a girl to my house, and I make a party in the house and everything, and you quit the girl and you go with another one, if you bring in somebody you should be sure you're going to marry her. [LAUGHS] And this one, when I brought her in he liked it. And I said, you come to Philadelphia. And I-- and I rent a hotel to stay for her. And if you like Philadelphia, I'd rather move-- you come to Philadelphia. It's a more life to raise children. New York, the apartment, with everything. She came to Philadelphia for day or two. And I went in, and I bought her a ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know her too long? How long did you know her?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: We decide this we're going to be-- we're going to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: After one month?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I cannot travel from Philadelphia day in, and I work hard and everything. And the girls used to tell her, he has friends. He go out dancing. Don't believe him. He called you on the phone and he said he's working and everything. He must have other girls. Don't believe him. But when she married me, she find out I was working very hard, seven days.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, yeah, her girlfriend come to visit us. And when we got engaged, in New York at Christmas, and we settled up the-- the wedding in April. And I came home. We got engaged. My brother was there. And her house was the engagement party, and I took pictures and everything. I was a photographers, movies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my landlady said, Sol, what are you going to do? Why are you waiting till April? Why don't you get married now, next month? You see what I mean? I called her back. And we made it February the 1st to get married.

speaker: interviewee
text: We get married. We went to Atlantic City. She never was in Atlantic City in February. It was beautiful, sunny, and everything. We stayed in a Jewish hotel and now they have same hotels in Miami. And we got married. And came back from the honeymoon. We was in the honeymoon in Atlantic City. My mother said-- my mother-in-law, she's gonna stay here. I took her furniture, and took her out, I say, you stay with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whoa, we started a war here, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I said, no Mom, we're not going to leave you by yourself here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you stay with me. And I rented an apartment in the beginning. I go away to work. The man was a Ukrainian. A Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, Ukrainian. He was a single man. We had-- he was working at nighttime. In daytime he was up. And I had an apartment above him. I came back. My wife was pregnant already. I came back. He's in my apartment, looking around in the refrigerator and everything. I said, you god damn it. I could kill you. Why are you going into my apartment? My wife would go up there, and you would there-- she would fall down from the steps. I moved out right away. And I bought a home. I bought a home, and my mother-in-law was very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: She saw Lois. When she die, it was just to me like my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what year did-- now what you did your wife die?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife died when we come back in '83.

speaker: interviewer
text: In '83, and she-- she died of cancer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She had an asthma attack. Lois was with me with the baby. The baby now is 13 years old, 14. The baby was nine months old. Lois was in my house. She was moving, from Long Island to Great Neck. And my wife used to help with moving and driving. This was Friday night. And she was with me with the baby. And the boy was with his father in-- in Long Island. And she got an attack at nighttime, till the police come in and the fire department and everything. She was fine. She got-- they give her oxygen. And Lois dressed her. And the police said, you follow me. We gonna examine her, and you take her home.

speaker: interviewee
text: We get to the hospital. They ask me, what is your wife's name? This. She die already. I almost died too. Had she had asthma before?

speaker: interviewee
text: An asthma attack. No, but she was in California. We lived-- it didn't bother her. The day she died, I was trying-- I was wild. I collapsed. They tried to-- to bring me back to life. And I called my younger daughter in California. Lois called her sister. What? She didn't believe it. And she called the-- the hospital-- and they tell her your mother die.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- and I went to the airport to pick her up. And after the funeral, people were making a party on my house. My daughter saw this. She never saw a funeral. My mother die? You're celebrating? You're making a party? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she collapsed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a-- where you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the funeral.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you at Lois's? Or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In my here-- in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lois was-- this house was not finished yet. Anyway, she had a nervous breakdown. She collapsed. I rushed her to the hospital. And she was two weeks in hospital. I almost lost her. My mother died. They're eating, making a party? And since then she never come back to the house. She was supposed to stay with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who? Lois was going to stay in your apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, sister. No, the sister. She was supposed to stay with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who, Brenda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was single. That's why I had an apartment for two, one side an apartment, and the other side me and my wife. And she was there, and everything. She left. She never come back. I still have her clothes in the closet.

speaker: interviewer
text: From 1983?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She went back to California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Anyway--

speaker: interviewee
text: She went-- she went back. She got married. I didn't know about it. I almost collapsed. If-- if-- when she came back to the funeral, she was already married.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was already married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she never told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never told me, because she was going with a Mexican. She was afraid. I told him, if he come into my house, get out of here. She's not for you. But anyway, she fooled me. She lied. And I give-- and I give her some money. And when-- when she-- when she was-- I looked on her pocketbook how money left, and I saw a certificate. She got married. I told Lois. Lois was-- unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was excited? She was happy, or sad?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Lois was almost-- out of her mind. Later on, we got used to it. What are you going to do? It's your daughter. She got pregnant. She had a baby. What are you going to do?

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of-- what do you think this particular grandson, in in particular, what do you think that-- now that you've accepted everybody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. He's going-- he's going to the temple. And my son-in-law wants to become a Jew. I said, you don't have to become a Jew, so long as you-- you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but it would be nice if he-- if he did. To be a full family--

words.text: did. To be a full family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He wants to become. I asked the rabbi. You know, she's raised him Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it would be nice if your son-in-law became a Jew. Then everybody would be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually he's gonna become a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: That will be great. That will be great.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is very devoted to her and everything. Look, what are you going to do? If my wife would be alive, she died. Find out that she's married this boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in the meantime, everybody's getting together. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We will love him, and everything. He's good to her. He makes a good living. So what are you going to do? They're young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if-- is there anything that you, during your years that you learned that you would-- or any kind of feeling as a result of the-- having survived the war that would like to give a message to your children or to your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I tell--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to tell them?

words.text: What would you like to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them what I went through. They know about it. And my younger daughter wants me to write a biography. I wrote it, but it's 20 pages in Polish. And I stopped it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But is there some sort of message that you would like to give them, something that you particularly--

speaker: interviewee
text: I give them a message--

speaker: interviewer
text: Learned about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would give them one message, not to worry about nothing. Live normal. And hard work never kills nobody. You have aggravation and stress is a killer. Control yourself, and be a human being, and help people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--
